en.extract:
The yellow-headed blackbird (Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus) is a medium-sized blackbird with a yellow head.
It is the only member of the genus Xanthocephalus. Their scientific name derives from the Greek word for yellow, xanthous, and the word for head, cephalus. 


Description
Yellow-headed blackbirds are relatively large blackbirds with large, yellow heads. They measure 8.3–10.2 in (21–26 cm) in length, with a wingspan of 16.5–17.3 in (42–44 cm) and a weight of 1.6–3.5 oz (44–100 g).
Adults have a pointed bill. The adult male is mainly black with a yellow head and breast; they have a white wing patch sometimes only visible in flight. The adult female is mainly brown with a dull yellow throat and breast. Immature members of both sexes are brown with duller yellow plumage compared to adult males. Immature males also have some white patches on the wing. Both sexes resemble the respective sexes of the smaller yellow-hooded blackbird of South America.


Behavior and ecology


Migration, habitat, and breeding
These birds migrate in the winter to the southwestern United States and Mexico. They often migrate in huge flocks with other species of birds. The only regions of the United States where these blackbirds are permanent residents are the San Joaquin Valley and the Lower Colorado River Valley of Arizona and California. It is an extremely rare vagrant to western Europe, with some records suspected to refer to escapes from captivity. When migrating, males and females travel separately. Males typically arrive at the breeding marshes 2–3 weeks before females during spring migration. Research suggests that females choose breeding sites based on the reproductive success (number of young per breeding female) of the site in previous years.
The breeding habitat of the yellow-headed blackbird are marshes in North America (mainly west of the Great Lakes), particularly in plants such as cattails (genus Typha), bulrush (genus Scirpus), and common reeds (genus Phragmites). The nest is built with and attached to marsh vegetation and is constructed over open water. They nest in colonies, often sharing their habitat closely with the red-winged blackbird (Agelaius phoeniceus). During the breeding and nesting season the males are very territorial and spend much of their time perched on reed stalks and displaying or chasing off intruders. 


Food
These birds eat seeds for the majority of the year and insects throughout the summer months. They forage in marshes, fields, or on the ground, sometimes catching insects in flight. Sunflowers are fairly popular amongst yellow-headed blackbirds in the Northern Great Plains, with a study indicating that males eat more sunflower than grains and females more grains than sunflower. Some methods of gathering food involve flipping over stones, catching insects from the top of water, and foraging. Foraging methods take place in uplands, with the flock taking a "rolling" formation in which birds fly from the back to the front of the flock to feed. Female yellow-headed blackbirds primarily feed their newly-hatched young insects from the order Odonata, which includes dragonflies and damselflies. Outside the nesting period, they often feed in flocks, often with related species.


Songs and calls
This bird's song resembles the grating of a rusty hinge. Male yellow-headed blackbirds have been observed to have two types of songs, an "accent song" and a "buzz song". The "buzz songs'' have much higher pitch than the accent song, and thus do not echo as well in the dense marshes they live in. For that reason, buzzing songs are typically done when communicating closer whereas accent songs are done to communicate with birds further in the marshes. Female birds have a song that is described to be similar to the male "buzz song", consisting of harsh grating or buzz sounds. Both sexes are also found to elicit harsh calling notes.


Natural threats
Yellow‐headed blackbirds have been found to be sensitive to nest predation risk, for example by marsh wrens (Cistothorus palustris), and alter their nest attendance behavior accordingly.
Raptors occasionally prey on yellow-headed blackbird flocks. For example, media outlets reported that on February 7, 2022, a flock of yellow-headed blackbirds migrating to Chihuahua, Mexico, were seen dropping dead in the early morning. According to experts, it is most likely that the birds were killed due to being flushed from above as a result of a predatory bird (presumably a raptor) chasing them, which caused some of them to fly into each other and crash into the ground and nearby buildings.


Conservation
Because yellow-headed blackbirds typically reside in wetlands, their population numbers depend on the conditions of the wetlands in which they reside. For instance, drainage projects, herbicides/pesticides, and other crop protection methods have impacts on the health of wetlands, and consequently, cause the bird population to fluctuate in number. Currently, yellow-headed blackbird numbers are stable and will likely remain that way in the long term.  


Gallery


References

Video of a female yellow-headed blackbird in Annapolis Valley, Nova Scotia, Canada, well outside its normal range.

The '''yellow-headed blackbird''' ('''''Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus''''') is a medium-sized [[icterid|blackbird]] with a yellow head.

It is the only member of the [[genus]] '''''Xanthocephalus'''''. Their scientific name derives from the Greek word for yellow, ''xanthous'', and the word for head, ''cephalus''.<ref name=":2" /> 

== Description ==
Yellow-headed blackbirds are relatively large blackbirds with large, yellow heads.<ref name=":2">Twedt, Daniel J. "Ecology of yellow-headed blackbirds." ''Ecology and Management of Blackbirds (Icteridae) in North America''. CRC Press, 2017. 43–64.</ref> They measure 8.3–10.2 in (21–26 cm) in length, with a wingspan of 16.5–17.3 in (42–44 cm) and a weight of 1.6–3.5 oz (44–100 g).<ref>{{cite web|title=Yellow-headed Blackbird Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Yellow-headed_Blackbird/id|access-date=2020-09-30|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref>

Adults have a pointed bill. The adult male is mainly black with a yellow head and breast; they have a white wing patch sometimes only visible in flight. The adult female is mainly brown with a dull yellow throat and breast. Immature members of both sexes are brown with duller yellow plumage compared to adult males. Immature males also have some white patches on the wing.<ref name=":0">{{cite web|title=Yellow-headed Blackbird Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Yellow-headed_Blackbird/id|access-date=2020-09-30|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref> Both sexes resemble the respective sexes of the smaller [[yellow-hooded blackbird]] of South America.

== Behavior and ecology ==

=== Migration, habitat, and breeding ===
These birds [[bird migration|migrate]] in the winter to the southwestern United States and Mexico. They often migrate in huge flocks with other species of birds. The only regions of the United States where these blackbirds are permanent residents are the [[San Joaquin Valley]] and the Lower [[Colorado River]] Valley of [[Arizona]] and [[California]]. It is an extremely rare vagrant to western Europe, with some records suspected to refer to escapes from captivity. When migrating, males and females travel separately. Males typically arrive at the breeding marshes 2–3 weeks before females during spring migration.<ref name=":1">Ellarson, ROBERT S. "The yellow-headed blackbird in Wisconsin." ''Passenger Pigeon'' 12 (1950): 99–109.</ref> Research suggests that females choose breeding sites based on the reproductive success (number of young per breeding female) of the site in previous years.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Ward|first=Michael P.|date=2005-10-01|title=Habitat selection by dispersing yellow-headed blackbirds: evidence of prospecting and the use of public information|journal=Oecologia|language=en|volume=145|issue=4|pages=650–657|doi=10.1007/s00442-005-0179-0|pmid=16007408|bibcode=2005Oecol.145..650W|s2cid=9352638|issn=1432-1939}}</ref>

The breeding [[Habitat (ecology)|habitat]] of the yellow-headed blackbird are [[Marsh|marshes]] in North America (mainly west of the [[Great Lakes]]), particularly in plants such as cattails (genus ''[[Typha]]''), bulrush (genus ''[[Scirpus]]''), and common reeds (genus ''[[Phragmites]]'').<ref name=":42">{{Cite journal|last=Nero|first=Robert W.|date=1963|title=Comparative Behavior of the Yellow-Headed Blackbird, Red-Winged Blackbird, and Other Icterids|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=75|issue=4|pages=376–413|jstor=4159213|issn=0043-5643}}</ref> The nest is built with and attached to marsh vegetation and is constructed over open water.<ref name=":1" /> They nest in [[Bird colony|colonies]], often sharing their habitat closely with the [[red-winged blackbird]] (''Agelaius phoeniceus''). During the [[breeding in the wild|breeding]] and [[nesting season]] the males are very territorial and spend much of their time perched on reed stalks and displaying or chasing off intruders. 

=== Food ===
These birds eat seeds for the majority of the year and insects throughout the summer months.<ref>{{cite web|title=Yellow-headed Blackbird Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Yellow-headed_Blackbird/lifehistory|access-date=2021-11-10|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref> They forage in marshes, fields, or on the ground, sometimes catching insects in flight. Sunflowers are fairly popular amongst yellow-headed blackbirds in the Northern Great Plains, with a study indicating that males eat more sunflower than grains and females more grains than sunflower.<ref>Twedt, Daniel J., William J. Bleier, and George M. Linz. "Geographic and temporal variation in the diet of Yellow-headed Blackbirds." ''The Condor'' 93.4 (1991): 975–986.</ref> Some methods of gathering food involve flipping over stones, catching insects from the top of water, and foraging. Foraging methods take place in uplands, with the flock taking a "rolling" formation in which birds fly from the back to the front of the flock to feed. Female yellow-headed blackbirds primarily feed their newly-hatched young insects from the order ''[[Odonata]]'', which includes dragonflies and damselflies.<ref name=":32">{{Cite journal|last=Ward|first=Michael P.|date=2005-10-01|title=Habitat selection by dispersing yellow-headed blackbirds: evidence of prospecting and the use of public information|journal=Oecologia|language=en|volume=145|issue=4|pages=650–657|doi=10.1007/s00442-005-0179-0|pmid=16007408|bibcode=2005Oecol.145..650W|s2cid=9352638|issn=1432-1939}}</ref> Outside the nesting period, they often feed in flocks, often with related species.

=== Songs and calls ===
This bird's song resembles the grating of a rusty hinge. Male yellow-headed blackbirds have been observed to have two types of songs, an "accent song" and a "buzz song". The "buzz songs<nowiki>''</nowiki> have much higher pitch than the accent song, and thus do not echo as well in the dense marshes they live in. For that reason, buzzing songs are typically done when communicating closer whereas accent songs are done to communicate with birds further in the marshes.<ref>{{cite web|date=2015-06-15|title=The Sweet Songs Of The Yellow-Headed Blackbird|url=https://www.mtpr.org/arts-culture/2015-06-15/the-sweet-songs-of-the-yellow-headed-blackbird|access-date=2021-11-13|website=Montana Public Radio|language=en}}</ref> Female birds have a song that is described to be similar to the male "buzz song", consisting of harsh grating or buzz sounds. Both sexes are also found to elicit harsh calling notes.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last=Nero|first=Robert W.|date=1963|title=Comparative Behavior of the Yellow-Headed Blackbird, Red-Winged Blackbird, and Other Icterids|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=75|issue=4|pages=376–413|jstor=4159213|issn=0043-5643}}</ref>

=== Natural threats ===
Yellow‐headed blackbirds have been found to be sensitive to nest predation risk, for example by [[Marsh wren|marsh wrens]] (''Cistothorus palustris)'', and alter their nest attendance behavior accordingly.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Behrens, C.|author2=Ruff, Z.J.|author3=Harms, T.M.|author4=Dinsmore, S.J.|year=2019|title=Predator density influences nest attendance of Yellow-headed Blackbirds ''Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus''|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ibi.12705|journal=Ibis|volume=161|issue=3|pages=679–685|doi=10.1111/ibi.12705|s2cid=92452532|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Raptors occasionally prey on yellow-headed blackbird flocks.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Behavior - Yellow-headed Blackbird - Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus - Birds of the World|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/yehbla/cur/behavior|access-date=2022-02-19|website=birdsoftheworld.org|doi=10.2173/bow.yehbla.01|s2cid=216373852|language=en|url-access=subscription}}</ref> For example, media outlets reported that on February 7, 2022, a flock of yellow-headed blackbirds migrating to Chihuahua, Mexico, were seen dropping dead in the early morning.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/15/mexico-birds-falling-sky/ |title=Viral video shows blackbirds falling from sky in Mexico |newspaper=The Washington Post |date=2022-02-15 |access-date=2022-06-11}}</ref> According to experts, it is most likely that the birds were killed due to being flushed from above as a result of a predatory bird (presumably a raptor) chasing them, which caused some of them to fly into each other and crash into the ground and nearby buildings.<ref>{{cite web|date=2022-02-14|title=Why did birds fall from sky in Mexico? Probably a predator, experts say|url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2022/feb/14/why-did-birds-fall-from-sky-in-mexico-probably-a-predator-experts-say-aoe|access-date=2022-02-19|website=the Guardian|language=en}}</ref>

== Conservation ==
Because yellow-headed blackbirds typically reside in wetlands, their population numbers depend on the conditions of the wetlands in which they reside.<ref name=":02">{{cite web|title=Yellow-headed Blackbird Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Yellow-headed_Blackbird/id|access-date=2020-09-30|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref> For instance, drainage projects, herbicides/pesticides, and other crop protection methods have impacts on the health of wetlands, and consequently, cause the bird population to fluctuate in number. Currently, yellow-headed blackbird numbers are stable and will likely remain that way in the long term.

zh.extract:
黄头黑鹂（学名：Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus）是雀形目拟鹂科的一种鸟类，属于单型的黄头黑鹂属（学名：Xanthocephalus）。
黄头黑鹂体重约62.68克，翼长约12.94厘米，喙宽度约6.6毫米，喙厚度约9.1毫米，嘴峰长约22.5毫米，跗蹠长约32.9毫米，尾长约10.19厘米。
黄头黑鹂具有部分的迁徙性，栖息在湖泊、沼泽等湿地环境，它们是植食性动物，主要以植物的种子或坚果为食。它们分布在加拿大南部到美国南部；冬季迁徙岛墨西哥中部越冬。
